literal: the doctrine of non-one-sidedness; from an-eka-anta ("not of one end or aspect") + vāda ("doctrine")
invariant_definition: The Jaina doctrine that no existent is exhausted by a single predication or standpoint. Its metaphysical basis is the Jaina account of reality as simultaneously enduring and changing: a thing is a *dravya* with persistent identity, but it is also a stream of *paryāyas* or modes. Because predication tracks some aspect, relation, time, or practical standpoint, any true judgment is true under a determinate *naya* rather than as an unqualified total description. *Anekānta-vāda* therefore presupposes the paired apparatus of *naya* (standpoint-analysis) and *syād-vāda* (conditional predication). It is not the claim that contradictions are simply true together, nor the modern thesis that all opinions are equally valid. The doctrine instead blocks one-sided metaphysical absolutization: the Nyāya realist, Buddhist momentarist, and Vedāntin monist are each said to seize one feature of things and elevate it into the whole account. The load-bearing commitment is ontological and logical together: reality is many-sided, so assertion must mark the aspect under which it speaks.

per_school.definition: Advaita rejects the Jaina conjunction of contrary predicates in one dharmin, since `sat` and `asat` cannot be simultaneously instantiated in the same locus any more than heat and cold. [cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.33-36] Śaṅkara further argues that once `anekāntatva` is universalized, even determinate cognition, scripture, and liberating practice lose fixity, so the position defeats `pramāṇa` rather than refining it. [cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.33-36]

Viśiṣṭādvaita rejects the Jaina claim that one vastu is affirmable as existent and non-existent through a sevenfold scheme, because mutually opposed predicates do not inhere together in a single thing. [cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/2.2.33-36] Rāmānuja accordingly distinguishes his own structured Brahman-world relation from Jaina `anekānta`: Brahman's relation to `cit` and `acit` is ordered through `śarīra-śarīrin`, not through oscillating assertion and negation of the same predicate. [cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/2.2.33-36]

per_school.definition: Nimbārka rejects the Jaina `saptabhaṅgī` because a single dharmin cannot bear `asti` and `nāsti` in the same respect, and a merely standpoint-driven vacillation would undercut determinate knowledge. [cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.2.33-36] He nonetheless distinguishes his own `svābhāvika-bhedābheda` from that target: Brahman and world are non-different through dependence and inseparability, while their difference remains intrinsic rather than viewpoint-conditional. [cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.2.33-36] [cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42]

Vallabha dismisses Jaina sevenfold predication as contradictory when imposed on one entity, and he rejects the appeal to `paryāya` because contraction and expansion of the self would still entail `vikāra`. [cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.2.33-36] Śuddhādvaita therefore secures plurality within non-duality through `avikṛta-pariṇāma`, not through aspect-indexed coexistence of opposed predicates. [cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.2.33-36] [cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26]

per_school.definition: In the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika materials on disk, `anekānta` is not the Jaina doctrine of many-sided predication but the defect of an inconclusive consideration whose force extends to opposed cases. [cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/2.1.20] [cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/3.2.17] Vātsyāyana explains that such `anekānta` arises when a dṛṣṭānta is advanced without a `viśeṣa-hetu`, so the example can be pressed on both sides and no determinate inference follows. [cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/2.1.20]

per_school.definition: In Umāsvāti's foundational presentation, an object is grasped through `pramāṇa` and `naya`, so cognition does not absolutize one unqualified predication but proceeds through disciplined standpoint-analysis. [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.6] [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.34] The ontological ground is that the real is marked by origination, cessation, and persistence together, which allows substantial identity and modal variation to be jointly affirmed without collapsing into sheer contradiction. [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.29]

per_school.not_applicable_reason: No on-disk Vaiyākaraṇa primary is available for this phase, and the school is not represented here with a substantive treatment of Jaina anekānta-vāda.

translator_note: "Non-absolutism" or "many-sidedness" is usually better than "relativism." The lazy gloss "relativism" imports modern epistemic permissiveness and suggests that contradictions are simply tolerated. Jaina usage is stricter: a predicate is valid only under a marked respect, and the metaphysical reason is the *dravya-paryāya* structure of the thing, not a modern sociology of viewpoints.
